disclaimer: THE SOURCE REGISTRY IS NOT HUMAN-VERIFIED. Each entry is a CANDIDATE official URL: our own crawler fetched it and read its title, and its `verification_status` says whether a named human has since confirmed it is the official page for that document class in that jurisdiction. `unverified` means nobody has. A machine cannot tell a state's real birth-certificate page from a convincing-looking one — it cannot even tell a live page from a bot-wall served with HTTP 200 — so do not rely on an unverified entry as authoritative guidance, and do not present it to anyone as one. What this tool does claim: this URL changed, and here is what changed in it. What it never claims: what the law is.

gaps.detail: Both Alaska DMV hosts 403 our descriptive User-Agent. We do not spoof a browser UA. The Alaska statutes/admin-code site (akleg.gov) answers us, but publishes only a table of contents whose deep links are #fragments — a fragment cannot scope a fetch, so watching it would claim a precision we do not have.

The Arkansas judiciary's host presents a chain our trust store cannot verify, and the Arkansas Code is not published as state-hosted HTML. Same refusal as above: a broken chain is the server's defect, and weakening our TLS verification to compensate would be ours.

Both present a chain our trust store cannot verify. We do not disable certificate verification. Arkansas does not publish its Code as state-hosted HTML (it is licensed to a commercial publisher), so there is no alternative official surface for this document class.

CDPHE 403s our UA (CloudFront). The Colorado Secretary of State's Code of Regulations — which publishes CDPHE's vital-statistics rules and would have been the honest alternative — presents a chain our trust store cannot verify. Both refusals, one gap.

403 to our UA (CloudFront). Colorado's statutes are published by the legislature only as year-stamped PDFs (crs2024-title-42.pdf), i.e. at a URL that is FROZEN once published: watching it could never detect a change, because next year's text appears at a different URL. A source that cannot drift is worse than no source — it is a wrong 'no change' with a green light on it.

jud.ct.gov completes no TLS handshake with the tool's OpenSSL 3.5 (SSLV3_ALERT_HANDSHAKE_FAILURE) while answering a legacy OpenSSL 1.1.1 client with 200 — 'it loads in my browser' is not evidence that this tool can watch it. The obvious alternative, the General Assembly's own site (cga.ct.gov), presents a chain our trust store cannot verify. Connecticut's court name-change process therefore has no honestly watchable official surface.

dccourts.gov 403s our descriptive UA on every path including the site root. NEWLY NAMED: this hole existed before today and was NOT in the old GAP list — the coverage gate now derives the gap list from the registry, so a silent thin spot fails the build instead of sitting there.

flcourts.gov is a client-rendered SPA AND its robots.txt disallows our UA from the form-download paths where the name-change forms live — either one alone would end it. The alternative official surface, the Legislature's statute page for Fla. Stat. 68.07, renders TODAY'S DATE into the page: it would produce a change record every single day, forever, whose diff is the date. `--twice` does not catch that (it is stable across two back-to-back fetches on the same day); reading the normalized text does, which is why we read it.

The Office of Motor Vehicles' public site is a JavaScript shell that normalizes to 2 passages — hashing it would watch a loading spinner, not a policy. dps.louisiana.gov's robots.txt disallows us. Louisiana's court name-change statute WAS recovered (see la-rs-13-4751); the OMV's licensing rules have no equivalent state-hosted HTML surface.

oscn.net — which is where Oklahoma publishes BOTH its court information and its statutes — serves `User-agent: * / Disallow: /`. It forbids all automated access to the entire site, and we honour that without appeal. The Legislature's own site (oklegislature.gov) presents a chain our trust store cannot verify. Removed, not spoofed.

Every South Dakota surface for this document class is client-rendered: sd.gov/dps is a 940KB ServiceNow portal whose text is 'Loading…', dps.sd.gov normalizes to 4 passages, and sdlegislature.gov (which would have been the statutory alternative) serves a page whose title is literally 'Loading...' and whose text is 6 passages. There is nothing here a hash can honestly watch.

The DMV and its parent agency both 403 our UA, and the Legislature's site — the alternative statutory surface — presents a chain our trust store cannot verify. Three hosts, three honest refusals, one gap.
